content.text: in Using Citations is the process of interpreting and displaying Claude's response output that contains both quoted text and citation metadata, where each claim is linked back to exact passages in source documents with location indices.
) alongside the actual quoted text from source documents, creating verifiable, trustworthy AI answers.
"Claude provides cited response. Responses may now include multiple text blocks where each text block can contain a claim that Claude is making and a list of citations."
"The methodology used a randomized controlled trial design [p. 45]."
"Claude Citations outputs the exact passage from the cited document as well as start and end indices referencing the slices of the original document (as a string) that contain the citations."
Method 1: Highlight Citations in Source Document

1. User selects question → View model-generated answer
2. Clicking answer → Highlights areas of document that Claude used
3. Scroll to bottom → Links to cited passages

# To add highlighting, iterate through citations from end to beginning
# to avoid interfering with citation start/end indices
for citation in reversed(citations):
    start = citation['start_char']
    end = citation['end_char']
    
    # Wrap highlighted text with <mark> tags
    highlighted_text = f"<mark>{original_text[start:end]}</mark>"
    full_text = full_text[:start] + highlighted_text + full_text[end:]
The methodology used a randomized controlled trial design [p. 45].

# Render citation links
for citation in response.citations:
    print(f"📄 {citation['cited_text']}")
    print(f"   Source: {citation['document_title']} [p. {citation['start_page_number']}]")

📄 "The methodology used a randomized controlled trial design."
   Source: research_paper.pdf [p. 45]

📄 "Key findings showed 25% improvement in outcomes."
   Source: research_paper.pdf [pp. 78-82]

Question: "What were the key findings?"

Model Answer:
"The methodology used a randomized controlled trial design [p. 45].
Key findings showed 25% improvement in outcomes [pp. 78-82]."

[Click to highlight source document]
[View highlighted passages]
[Jump to cited location]

"To make the above UX work as rendered HTML, we need to first add HTML markup to the raw markdown string before rendering, since the cited passages and document indices would no't match the content after rendering the markdown as HTML."
Every claim linked to source text
"With Anthropic's Citations, we reduced source hallucinations and formatting issues from 10% to 0% and saw a 20% increase in references per response."

Summary:
- Defendant filed motion on March 15 [p. 12]
- Court granted motion on April 2 [p. 18]
- Settlement amount: $500,000 [p. 25]

Q: What were Q3 revenue figures?
A: Q3 revenue was $1.2M, representing 15% growth from Q2 [pp. 15-16]

Q: How do I reset my password?
A: Go to Settings → Account → Security → Reset Password [Manual p. 8, FAQ p. 3]
Add highlighting to markdown before HTML conversion
Iterate citations from end to beginning
"We need to first add HTML markup to the raw markdown string before rendering, since the cited passages and document indices would no longer match the content after rendering the markdown as HTML."
When working with reading cited responses, following established best practices ensures reliability, maintainability, and optimal performance.
Start simple and iterate — begin with the minimum viable implementation before adding complexity.
Test with representative inputs — ensure your test cases cover edge cases, not just the happy path.
Monitor performance — track latency, token usage, and error rates in production environments.
Version your prompts and configurations — treat prompt text like source code with proper version control.
Document your design decisions — record why you chose specific approaches for future maintainers.
Plan for failure — implement graceful degradation when the AI component is unavailable or returns unexpected results.
Review outputs regularly — periodically audit what the system produces to catch quality drift over time.
These pitfalls frequently trip up developers working with reading cited responses. Knowing them in advance saves significant debugging time.
Assuming deterministic output — language models are probabilistic; the same input can produce different outputs across calls. Always validate the structure of responses.
Ignoring token limits — exceeding context window limits causes truncation or errors. Track token counts, especially in multi-turn conversations.
Over-engineering early — building complex infrastructure before validating basic functionality wastes effort. Get a working prototype first.
Hardcoding prompts in code — embed prompts in configuration files or databases so non-developers can iterate on them.
Skipping error handling — API calls fail due to rate limits, timeouts, or content policy violations. Always wrap calls in try/catch with appropriate retry logic.
As your usage of reading cited responses scales, these performance factors become increasingly important.
Latency budget — typical API latency ranges from 1-30 seconds depending on model size and output length. Design your UX around streaming to provide early feedback.
Concurrency limits — each API tier has request-per-minute (RPM) and token-per-minute (TPM) limits. Implement request queuing for burst traffic.
Prompt caching — use the cache_control breakpoint on stable, repeated prompt prefixes to reduce costs by up to 90% and latency by 85%.
Batching — for non-interactive workloads, the Message Batches API processes up to 10,000 requests asynchronously at 50% cost reduction.
Model selection — use Claude Haiku for high-volume, low-complexity tasks and Claude Sonnet or Opus for complex reasoning where quality matters more than speed.
Follow these best practices when working with reading cited responses to maximize reliability and performance.
Start simple and iterate incrementally — validate your core approach before adding complexity.
Test with realistic inputs — use representative examples from your actual use case, not just toy examples.
Monitor in production — track latency, error rates, and output quality with structured logging and metrics.
Version control your configurations — treat prompts and settings like code with proper change tracking.
Handle errors gracefully — implement retry logic with exponential backoff for transient API errors.
Set appropriate timeouts — long-running requests should have explicit timeout limits to prevent hangs.
Review outputs regularly — periodically audit produced content to catch quality drift as models are updated.
Common Mistakes and How to Avoid Them
These pitfalls catch most developers off guard. Learn from them upfront.
Treating LLM output as deterministic — outputs vary between runs. Always validate structure and handle unexpected formats.
Not accounting for token limits — conversations grow over time. Implement context truncation strategies before hitting limits.
Over-engineering before validation — build the simplest thing that works, then optimize based on real measurements.
Ignoring rate limits — API throttling causes failures under load. Implement request queuing and rate-limit-aware retry logic.
Hardcoding prompts in source code — separate prompt text from code to enable rapid iteration without deployments.
At scale, reading cited responses introduces specific performance challenges that require deliberate design decisions.
Latency: Claude Haiku responds in 1-5 seconds; Sonnet in 5-15 seconds; Opus in 15-30+ seconds. Choose the right model for your latency budget.
Throughput: Use async clients (AsyncAnthropic in Python, async/await in TypeScript) for concurrent requests rather than sequential blocking calls.
Cost optimization: Prompt caching reduces costs by 90% on repeated prefixes. The Message Batches API gives 50% discount for offline workloads.
Streaming: Use streaming responses to reduce perceived latency — users see output immediately rather than waiting for complete generation.
Connection pooling: Reuse HTTP connections with a persistent client instance rather than creating a new client per request.
Production deployments require attention to security boundaries and reliability engineering.
Never log API keys — rotate credentials immediately if they appear in logs, error messages, or version control history.
Validate and sanitize inputs — prompt injection attacks can exfiltrate data or override instructions if user inputs are embedded directly.
Implement circuit breakers — automatically stop sending requests when error rates exceed threshold to prevent cascading failures.
Audit all AI-generated actions — log every API call, input, and output with correlation IDs for debugging and compliance.

Reading Cited Responses is a foundational aspect of building reliable Claude-powered applications. The patterns covered here — careful validation, incremental iteration, proper error handling, and performance-aware design — apply broadly across all Claude integrations. Master these fundamentals and you will be well-equipped to tackle more complex scenarios as your application evolves.
